title: Output Prediction
description: Master the art of mentally tracing Java code and predicting exact console output — a critical skill for CSE110 exams. This chapter covers print vs println vs printf, expression evaluation order, type promotion, and the tricky edge cases that trip up students every time.

sections.content: In CSE110 exams, a large portion of marks come from two skills: **predicting what a program prints** and **finding errors in code**. These are not guessing games — they follow strict, deterministic rules. Every character Java prints is the result of a rule. This chapter teaches you to **see code the way the JVM sees it**.

sections.label: Exam Strategy
sections.content: Always trace code **line by line**, maintaining a small table of variable values in your head (or on paper). Never jump ahead. The answer is always in the details.
Java provides three core methods for console output. Understanding their **exact differences** is non-negotiable.

sections.label: Critical Distinction
sections.content: `System.out.println()` with **no arguments** prints an empty line (just a newline). This is a common exam trick — it doesn't print `null` or an error, just a blank line.
The `+` operator is the most **context-sensitive** operator in Java. Its behavior changes completely based on the types of its operands. The golden rule: **evaluation is strictly left-to-right**.

sections.title: The + Operator — Context Demonstration

sections.label: The char Trap
sections.content: When you write `'A' + 1`, Java treats `'A'` as its ASCII value (65), so the result is **66** (an integer), NOT the string `"A1"`. To get `"A1"`, you need `"" + 'A' + 1` or `Character.toString('A') + 1`.

Java performs **integer division** when both operands are integers — the result is **truncated** (not rounded) toward zero. The type of the result follows the **type promotion hierarchy**: `byte → short → int → long → float → double`.

sections.title: Division and Promotion Rules

sections.label: Silent Truncation Bug
sections.content: Writing `double result = a / b;` where `a` and `b` are both `int` does **NOT** give a decimal result. The integer division `a/b` happens first, producing `2`, and then `2` is promoted to `2.0`. To fix it: `double result = (double) a / b;`
The `++` and `--` operators have **prefix** (pre) and **postfix** (post) forms. The difference is critical when used inside expressions or print statements.

sections.title: Pre vs Post Increment Deep Dive

sections.content: The `printf` method uses **format specifiers** to control how values are displayed. These are frequently tested in CSE110.

sections.title: printf in Action

sections.content: Try each exercise yourself before clicking **Reveal**. These are designed at CSE110 exam difficulty. Cover the answer and genuinely try first — that's how the skill is built.

sections.title: Exercise 1: The Classic + Operator Trap
sections.explanation: Line 1: String context from start, so `"Result: " + 3` = `"Result: 3"`, then `+ 4` = `"Result: 34"`. Line 2: `3 + 4 = 7` first (both ints), then `7 + " Result"` = `"7 Result"`. Line 3: `"A" + 'B'` = `"AB"` (string context), then `+ 2` = `"AB2"`. Line 4: `'A' + 'B'` = `65 + 66 = 131` (both chars add as ints!), then `131 + "2"` = `"1312"`.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Pre/Post Increment in print
sections.explanation: Start: x=10. `x++` prints 10, x becomes 11. `++x` increments to 12 first, prints 12. `x--` prints 12, x becomes 11. `--x` decrements to 10 first, prints 10. Final print: x=10.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Integer Division Chain
sections.explanation: Line 1: Integer division 10/3 = 3. Line 2: 10 % 3 = 1. Lines 3 & 4: One operand is double so result is double. Note the repeated 3 and trailing 5 — that's floating-point representation. Line 5: `a/b` is integer division (3) FIRST, then promoted to `3.0` when stored.

sections.title: Exercise 4: println() vs print() spacing
sections.explanation: `print` does NOT add a newline. `println()` alone adds just a newline (blank line effect here closes the AB line). `println("C")` prints C then newline. `print("D")` + `println("E")` = DE then newline. Final `print("F")` has no newline — cursor stays on same line after F.

sections.title: Exercise 5: printf with format specifiers
sections.explanation: Line 1: straightforward substitution. Line 2: `%.2f` rounds to 2 decimal places. Line 3: `%10d` = right-aligned in width 10 (8 spaces + 42). Line 4: `%-10d` = left-aligned in width 10, then `|`. Line 5: `%05d` = zero-padded to width 5.

sections.title: Exercise 6: Type casting chain (Tricky!)
sections.explanation: A: `(int)9.99` truncates to `9`. B: `3.9+4.9=8.8`, then `(int)8.8=8`. C: `(int)3.9=3` and `(int)4.9=4`, so `3+4=7`. D: Tricky! `byte` range is -128 to 127. 200 in binary overflows: `200 - 256 = -56` (two's complement wrap-around).

items: **`print`** — no newline. **`println`** — adds `\n`. **`printf`** — formatted, no auto newline.
**`+` operator**: left-to-right. If either side is a `String`, it's **concatenation**. Otherwise, arithmetic.
**`char` arithmetic**: `'A' + 1 = 66` (int). Use `"" + 'A'` to force string context.
**Integer division**: `7/2 = 3` (truncated). Cast at least one operand to `double` for decimal result.
**`x++`** — use value, THEN increment. **`++x`** — increment FIRST, then use value.
**`%f`** gives 6 decimal places by default. Use `%.2f` for 2 decimal places.
**`(byte)200 = -56`** — narrow casting can overflow and produce negative numbers.
